articles.title: Helen Webley-Brown
articles.contents:
Helen Webley-Brown
          
          
          
          
          
          About
          Research
          CV
          ☰
          
          
      
        
          
            
              
            
              Helen Webley-Brown
            
            
              
                
            
          
        
        
        
          
            I am a S.M. student in Political Science at MIT with
            specializations in American Politics and Quantitative
            Methods. I am also an affiliate of the Center for American Political
            Studies at Harvard University. In 2022, I graduated
            from Washington University in St. Louis with a A.B. in
            Political Science. This cycle, I will be applying to
            Ph.D. programs.
            Although my research focuses on the US, I grew up in
            London, England. I moved across the pond for college via the
            US-UK Fulbright Commission’s wonderful Sutton Trust US
            Programme. My personal background has greatly
            shaped my commitment to inclusive pedagogy, social mobility,
            and widening access to higher education.
            Currently, my research agenda fits into two broad
            buckets:
            The socio-political effects of interacting with local
            institutions of social control, focusing on the criminal legal system and public housing authorities.
            How emerging
            technologies change the way people experience and
            perceive the state.
            At the intersection of this, I am interested in the
            participatory implications of tools like pretrial electronic
            monitors and ShotSpotter in policing, and Landlord Tech in
            subsidized housing. Parallel to studies of demobilization, I
            also ask what fosters successful collective action in these
            contentious spaces? That is, under what conditions can
            marginalized communities carve out democratic practices
            within systems that control, surveil, or punish them? I have
            two early-stage projects that grapple with this question:
            one uses topic modelling to analyze Community Control Over
            Police Surveillance (CCOPS) legislation, while the other
            leverages a novel dataset to examine resident council
            emergence in public housing developments.
          
        
      
    

    
      
        
          
            
              
            
              Helen Webley-Brown
            
            
              
                
                                  
              
            
            
              I am a S.M. student in Political Science at MIT with
              specializations in American Politics and Quantitative
              Methods. I am also an affiliate of the Center for American Political
              Studies at Harvard University. In 2022, I
              graduated from Washington University in St. Louis with a
              A.B. in Political Science. This cycle, I will be applying
              to Ph.D. programs.
              Although my research focuses on the US, I grew up in
              London, England. I moved across the pond for college via
              the US-UK Fulbright Commission’s wonderful Sutton Trust US
              Programme. My personal background has greatly
              shaped my commitment to inclusive pedagogy, social
              mobility, and widening access to higher education.
              Currently, my research agenda fits into two broad
              buckets:
              The socio-political effects of interacting with local
              institutions of social control, focusing on the criminal legal system and public housing authorities.
              How emerging
              technologies change the way people experience and
              perceive the state.
              At the intersection of this, I am interested in the
              participatory implications of tools like pretrial
              electronic monitors and ShotSpotter in policing, and
              Landlord Tech in subsidized housing. Parallel to studies
              of demobilization, I also ask what fosters successful
              collective action in these contentious spaces? That is,
              under what conditions can marginalized communities carve
              out democratic practices within systems that control,
              surveil, or punish them? I have two early-stage projects
              that grapple with this question: one uses topic modelling
              to analyze Community Control Over Police Surveillance
              (CCOPS) legislation, while the other leverages a novel
              dataset to examine resident council emergence in public
              housing developments.

articles.title: Helen Webley-Brown
articles.contents:
Helen Webley-Brown
          
          
          
          
          
          About
          Research
          CV
          ☰
          
          
      
        
          
            Helen Webley-Brown
          
          
            
              Hello! I am a final-year S.M. student in Political
              Science at MIT with specializations in American Politics
              and Quantitative Methods. My research focuses on carceral
              politics and the use of emerging tech in subnational
              government services.
            
            
              Hello! I am a final-year S.M. student in Political
              Science at MIT with specializations in American Politics
              and Quantitative Methods. My research focuses on carceral
              politics and the use of emerging tech in subnational
              government services.

articles.title: Research
articles.contents:
Conference Papers
Gupta, A., Wu, V., Webley-Brown, H., King, J., Ho, D. (2023). The Privacy-Bias Tradeoff: Data Minimization and Racial Disparity Assessments in U.S. Government. In Proceedings of the 2023 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT ’23).
 Best Paper Award – FAccT 2023
 Student Paper Award – Privacy Papers for Policymakers (PPPM) ; Future of Privacy Forum
 Policy Brief – Institute on Human-Centered
Artificial Intelligence (HAI)
Works in Progess
Democracy Under Surveillance: The Effect of Pretrial Electronic Monitoring on Voting Behavior.
We’ve Got Democracy at Home: Resident Council Emergence in Public Housing Developments.
Don’t Shoot The Messenger: Can Source Attribution Moderate the Effect of Police Violence News on Trust in Police?
Why Watch The Watchers? Frame Utilization In Community Control Over Police Surveillance (CCOPS) Legislation.
